FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study 5.1 release, 21 U.S. sites, baseline N~12,000, ages 9-10; explicitly stated as U.S. ABCD Study throughout methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using parallel-process latent growth curve models on ABCD data across three time points (Y0, Y2, Y4).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed empirical article in Journal of Research on Adolescence; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media hours (time on social networking sites, hours/day, self-reported) is a named SM variable. Numeric slope-slope path estimates reported: s_sm -> s_cryst: beta = -.36, p < .001; s_sm -> s_flanker: beta = -.09, p = .031; s_sm -> s_pattern: beta = .11, p = .001 (Table 3). Other side: crystallized ability, Flanker inhibitory control/attention, and Pattern Comparison processing speed -- all individual-trait cognitive constructs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple standardized path coefficients (beta) with p-values and 95% CIs reported in Tables 2-3 for SM-hours <-> cognitive trajectory associations.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential parallel-process LGCMs with reported path coefficients, SEs, p-values, and CIs constitute a full quantitative inferential analysis.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional parallel-process design: social media slope predicts cognitive slopes AND cognitive intercepts predict social media slope (e.g., i_cryst -> s_sm: beta = .01, p = .665; i_pattern -> s_sm: beta = .06, p = .001). SM appears as both IV and DV within the same models, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: Study examines longitudinal coupling between social media hours trajectory and crystallized abilities, inhibitory control/attention, and processing speed trajectories using PP-LGCMs on ABCD 5.1 data across Y0-Y4.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study release 4.0, 21 U.S. sites, N=8,699, ages 9-10 at baseline; explicitly described as U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis applying target trial emulation (TTE) and G-formula to ABCD (and Generation R) observational data to estimate effects of hypothetical interventions on internalizing symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate with a phone/SM-specific variable label found anywhere in the paper. The exposure throughout is undifferentiated "recreational screen time" (ST) measured as total daily hours of recreational screen use by parent report, with no breakdown by device or platform. Step 2: The ST composite is not exclusively phone/SM items -- the Discussion explicitly acknowledges 'screen time measurement is complex, and it is composed of different activities which may have distinct effects' (p. 263) and cites studies distinguishing computer use, TV use, and gaming. The ABCD measure bundles all recreational screen activities into a single daily-hours figure with no phone/SM isolable component. No per-device or per-platform breakout exists in main tables, supplementary tables, or sensitivity analyses. Fails Step 2 as multi-activity composite with non-phone/non-SM ingredients not separately broken out.

notes: Screen time operationalized as total recreational daily hours (parent report) without phone/SM isolation. Multi-cohort study (ABCD + Generation R). FT-IC2 fails at Step 2 due to undifferentiated multi-activity screen time composite.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study 5.0 release, 21 U.S. sites, N=11,819, baseline ages 9-10; explicitly stated as U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using random-intercepts cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) across 4 waves of ABCD data (T1-T4).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in European Child and Adolescent Psychiatry; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: OSA (online social activity) time is constructed exclusively from "texting" + "visiting social networking sites" + "video chatting" -- all qualifying SM/phone items. Numeric cross-lagged path estimates reported: OSA time (T2->T3) -> ADHD problems beta = 0.058* (Fig. 1); OSA time (T3->T4) -> ADHD problems beta = 0.074** (Fig. 1); sex-stratified paths in Table 3 (e.g., females T2->T3: beta = 0.094, p = .023). Other side: ADHD problems measured by CBCL attention problems subscale T-scores -- an individual-trait behavioral/psychiatric construct.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple standardized path coefficients (beta), SEs, 95% CIs, and p-values reported in Figure 1, Table 2 (bivariate correlations), and Table 3 (sex-stratified cross-lagged estimates).

FT_IC4.evidence: RI-CLPMs with full inferential statistics (MLR estimator, FIML) constitute a quantitative inferential analysis; numeric estimates for the OSA <-> ADHD relationship are reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional cross-lagged design: OSA time predicts subsequent ADHD problems AND ADHD problems predict subsequent OSA time in the same RI-CLPM (e.g., ADHD T1->T2 -> OSA time beta = -0.006 females, -0.057 males; Table 3). SM/OSA appears as both IV and DV, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: OSA composite (texting + social networking + video chatting) is exclusively phone/SM items. Bidirectional RI-CLPM across 4 ABCD waves. Sex moderates the OSA->ADHD effect (significant only for girls); no moderation by content type (video vs. image).
